concept.title: Edge Delivery Services Overview
concept.shortdesc: This concept topic provides an overview of Adobe Edge Delivery Services (EDS) and how it integrates with AEM Guides for fast, modern content delivery.

section.title: What is EDS?
section.p: Adobe Edge Delivery Services is a fast, modern web delivery platform. It serves content from a GitHub repository through a globally distributed edge network. The system is built around three core ideas: GitHub-based authoring, markdown content, and block-based UI components.
For step-by-step setup instructions, see Configuring an EDS Output Preset.

ul.li: Lightning-fast page loads via global edge caching.
Content stored as markdown in a Git repository.
Block-based component system for UI flexibility.
Seamless integration with AEM Guides for DITA content.

ol.li: HTML5 — the primary output for EDS sites.
Native PDF — via Adobe's native PDF generator.

dlentry.dd: A logical grouping of content, separated by --- in markdown.
A reusable UI component, encoded as a markdown table whose first row contains the block name.
The file that tells aem.live where to fetch site content from.

section.title: Performance comparison
section.p: The following table compares EDS to traditional CMS delivery:

section.title: Code example
section.p: A typical fstab.yaml file looks like this:

section.title: Important notes
section.note: This is a regular note. Use it to draw attention to important but non-critical information.
Do not delete fstab.yaml from your repository — aem.live requires it for site registration.
Use the AEM Sidekick browser extension to publish individual pages without redeploying the whole site.
Changes to fstab.yaml after AEM Code Sync is installed may not be picked up.
Always commit a valid mountpoint before installing the AEM Code Sync GitHub App on your repository.

section.title: External references
section.p: For more information, see Adobe's aem.live documentation or the AEM Guides EDS boilerplate repository on GitHub.

section.title: Quote
section.lq: EDS isn't just a delivery platform — it's a complete reimagining of how content flows from author to reader, powered by edge computing and modern web standards.
